themes.heading: Muut turvallisuustason tiedot
themes.text: Vältä kaikkea matkustamista Kibiran kansallispuistossa. Kansallispuisto ulottuu Bubanzan ja Citiboken provinssien lisäksi Kayanzan ja Muramvyan provinsseihin. Opposition kannattajien ja turvallisuusjoukkojen välillä on tapahtunut yhteenottoja. Kansainväliseen terrorismiin liittyvät iskut ovat mahdollisia. Burundi on osallistunut Somalian rauhanturvaoperaatioihin ja islamistiryhmä Al-Shabaab on uhannut maata iskuilla.

themes.heading: Ajankohtaista
themes.text: Toukokuussa 2026 Maailman terveysjärjestö (WHO) julisti Ebola-epidemian Kongon demokraattisen tasavallan ja Ugandan alueilla. Aiheesta voi lukea täältä. Epidemia saattaa aiheuttaa lyhyellä varoitusajalla rajoituksia mm. rajanylityksissä. Turvallisuustilanne Kongossa on epävakaa. Se saattaa aiheuttaa levottomuuksia myös Burundin puolella. Turvallisuustilanne voi muuttua nopeasti. UM:n matkustustiedotteet ovat suosituksia Vastaat matkapäätöksestäsi aina itse. Paikallisilla viranomaisilla on ensisijainen vastuu kaikkien maan alueella oleskelevien ihmisten turvallisuudesta ja hätätilanteissa etsintä- ja pelastustoimista. Kriisitilanteissa noudata paikallisten viranomaisten ohjeita. Seuraa uutisointia tiedotusvälineissä sekä tiedotusta edustuston verkkosivuilla ja sosiaalisen median kanavissa. Ulkoministeriö ja Suomen ulkomaanedustustot avustavat suomalaisia konsulipalvelulain mukaisesti. Mikäli Euroopan unionin ulkopuolisessa kohdemaassa ei ole Suomen edustustoa, voit kääntyä muiden EU-maiden edustustojen puoleen. Matkatoimiston järjestämällä matkalla olevaa avustaa ensisijaisesti matkanjärjestäjä. Konsulipalvelulaki Konsulisuojeludirektiivi Laki matkapalveluyhdistelmistä Suosittelemme aina matkavakuutuksen ottamista. Tarkista ennen matkaasi matkavakuutuksesi kattavuus ja voimassaolo erityisesti jos sinulla on jokin sairaus, olet raskaana, matkasi suuntautuu vaarallisille alueille tai harrastat riskialttiita lajeja, Sairastuneen tai vainajan kotiuttaminen on kallista ja vaikea järjestää ilman kattavaa matkavakuutusta, eikä hoitoa aina saa ulkomailla ilman maksusitoumusta tai ennakkomaksua. Tee matkustusilmoitus osoitteessa https://matkustusilmoitus.fi/

themes.heading: Yleinen turvallisuustilanne
themes.text: Yleinen turvallisuustilanne on heikko. Maassa tehtiin epäonnistunut vallankaappausyritys toukokuussa 2015. Turvallisuustilanne Kongon demokraattisen tasavallan ja Ruandan raja-alueilla on edelleen epävakaa, ja aseellisia yhteenottoja esiintyy. Aseelliset ryöstöt pankkeihin ja rahanvaihtopisteisiin ovat mahdollisia. Turvallisuustilanne saattaa muuttua nopeasti. Poliittisen väkivallan uhka saattaa kohota mm. vaalien alla. Noudata paikallisviranomaisten ohjeita ja seuraa paikallisia tiedotusvälineitä.

themes.heading: Rikollisuus
themes.text: Maassa on paljon aseita ja aseistettuja kapinallis- ja rikollisryhmiä. Liikuttaessa ulkona on syytä olla tarkkaavainen. Varkaat iskevät varsinkin nostettaessa rahaa automaateista ja tilanteisiin voi liittyä väkivaltaa. Kävellen liikkuminen pimeällä on vaarallista, eikä autollakaan tule pimeällä liikkua kaupunkien ulkopuolella. Väkijoukkoja tulee välttää. Uuden passin hankkiminen edellyttää poliisiraportin tekoa, asiointia suurlähetystössä sekä uuden kohdemaan viisumin hankkimista. Tallenna kopiot passistasi, viisumistasi sekä viisumin maksukuitista sähköpostiisi, jotta tarvittaessa uuden passin ja viisumin hankinta helpottuu. Jos passisi katoaa tai varastetaan, tee siitä ilmoitus paikalliselle poliisiasemalle. Mikäli kohdemaassa ei ole Suomen edustustoa, hae hätämatkustusasiakirjaa (ETD = Emergency Travel Document) toisen EU-maan edustustosta. Ota kopiot tärkeimmistä asiapapereista kuten passista ja lentolipuista. Säilytä ne erillään alkuperäisistä.

themes.heading: Liikenne
themes.text: Liikennekulttuuri on kohtalaista, mutta autoilijoiden on syytä olla varovaisia. Bujumburassa liikenne on ajoittain sekavaa ja maaseudun tiet ovat kukkulaisen maaston vuoksi kapeita ja kiemuraisia. Suuret tiet ovat päällystettyjä.

themes.heading: Terveys
themes.text: Mpox–tartuntatautia (ns. apinarokko) esiintyy edelleen osissa Burundia. WHO on raportoinut uudentyyppisestä verenvuotokuumeesta Bubanzan alueella Mpandassa. Lisätietoja on saatavilla mm. Euroopan tautienehkäisy- ja valvontakeskuksen ECDC:n verkkosivuilla. Terveyspalvelujen taso on heikko. Burundissa on pitkään jatkunut koleraepidemia. Malariaa ja dengue-kuumetta esiintyy maanlaajuisesti. Burundin lähialueilla esiintyy ajoittain tartuntatautiepidemioita, jotka saattavat vaikuttaa myös rajanylityksiin. Maailman terveysjärjestön sekä Terveyden ja hyvinvoinnin laitoksen verkkosivuilta saa lisätietoa mm. Ebola-viruksesta ja M-rokosta. Selvitä ennakkoon matkakohteesi tartuntatautitilanne ja rokotussuositukset. Löydät ne seuraavien viranomaisten verkkosivuilta: Matkailijan terveysopas Maailman terveysjärjestö WHO Euroopan tartuntatautivirasto

themes.heading: Maan lait ja käytännöt
themes.text: Burundin lain mukaan jokaisen on pystyttävä kysyttäessä todistamaan henkilöllisyytensä. Valokuvaamiseen ja videokuvaamiseen liittyy monissa maissa rajoituksia. Mahdollisia kuvauskieltoja on syytä noudattaa. Yleensä esim. sotilaallisten laitosten, lentokenttien tai satamien valokuvaaminen ja videokuvaaminen on kielletty, ja kielto koskee myös ilmakuvausta drooneilla. Myös droonien maahantuonti voi vaatia erillisiä lupaprosesseja. Varmista tarvittaessa rajoitukset etukäteen paikallisviranomaisilta. Kiellettyjä kohteita kuvaava henkilö saatetaan ottaa kiinni tai pidättää, ja kuvauslaitteet saatetaan tuhota tai takavarikoida. Huumeiden hallussapidosta, käytöstä ja salakuljetuksesta annettavat tuomiot ovat ankaria. Päihteiden vaikutuksen alaisena ajamisesta voidaan pidättää välittömästi. Homoseksuaalisuus on rikos, josta on säädetty sakko- ja vankeusrangaistus.

themes.heading: Maahantulosäännökset
themes.text: UM ei vastaa vieraiden valtioiden maahantulosäännöksistä, kuten passin voimassaoloa koskevista ehdoista tai viisumivelvollisuudesta. Varmista ajantasaiset maahantulosäädökset kohdemaan viranomaisilta, esimerkiksi maan lähimmästä edustustosta. Lue lisää Suomen kansalaisen matkustusasiakirjoista osoitteessa https://um.fi/passi/

themes.heading: Sicherheit & Kriminalität
themes.text: Hohes Sicherheitsrisiko (Sicherheitsstufe 3 von 4) im ganzen Land. Von nicht unbedingt notwendigen Reisen wird daher abgeraten. Es kommt immer wieder zu Gefechten zwischen Opposition und Regierungsmilizen bzw. der Armee, besonders in den an die Demokratische Republik Kongo angrenzenden Provinzen. Bei Reisen in diese Provinzen (Cibitoke, Bubanza) – wie auch ins Landesinnere – ist besondere Vorsicht geboten. Gefahr terroristischer Anschläge im ganzen Land, informieren Sie sich genau über die Sicherheitslage vor Ort, meiden Sie unbedingt größere Menschenansammlungen und Demonstrationen. Hohe Kriminalität sowohl in der Hauptstadt Bujumbura als auch in ländlichen Gebieten, Autofahrten in der Nacht sollten unbedingt vermieden werden. Vor allem in den Außenbezirken aber auch im Zentrum Bujumburas sollten auch kürzere Strecken bei Dunkelheit nicht zu Fuß zurückgelegt werden. Die Landgrenze zu Ruanda ist bis auf weiteres geschlossen. Notrufnummern: Ambulanz: 22 27 56 32 oder 22 27 56 36 (Hospital Bumerec); 22 24 50 00 (KIRA Hospital) Feuerwehr: 113 Polizei: 112 Zivilschutz: 22 24 19 77

themes.heading: Einreise & Ausreise
themes.text: Visumpflicht: Ja Visum erhältlich: Visum kann online unter folgendem LINK beantragt werden. Weiterführende Informationen kann Ihnen die Botschaft der Republik Burundi in Berlin erteilen. Reisedokumente: Reisepass Passgültigkeit: Zumindest 6 Monate bei Einreise, 5 freie Seiten eAusweise: Elektronische Ausweise (ID Austria) werden derzeit ausschließlich in Österreich akzeptiert. Cremefarbiger Notpass: Wird nicht akzeptiert Konventions- oder Fremdenpass: Wenden Sie sich bitte an die weiter unten angeführte „ausländische Vertretung in Österreich“. Sonstiges: Nachweis der Wiederausreise (Rückflug- oder Weiterreiseticket) sowie genügend Geldmittel für den Aufenthalt Minderjährige: Für Minderjährige (bis 18 Jahre), die ohne Begleitung der gesetzlichen Vertreter oder mit nur einem gesetzlichen Vertreter verreisen, wird – zusätzlich zum eigenen Reisepass – auch eine Einverständniserklärung für die Reise benötigt. Dieser Vollmacht sollte eine Kopie der Geburtsurkunde des bzw. der Minderjährigen sowie eine Kopie der Reisepässe der gesetzlichen Vertreter angeschlossen sein. Bei unterschiedlichen Nachnamen empfiehlt sich auch die Mitnahme der Heiratsurkunde der Eltern. Muster für eine Einverständniserklärung finden Sie auf der Seite des ÖAMTC und des ARBÖ. Diebstahl/Verlust des Reisedokuments: Von der Verwendung gestohlener oder verlorener und wieder gefundener Reisedokumente wird abgeraten, auch wenn die Anzeige bei der zuständigen Behörde bereits widerrufen wurde, da Probleme an der Grenze bis zur Einreiseverweigerung nicht ausgeschlossen werden können.

themes.heading: Einfuhr & Ausfuhr
themes.text: Die Einfuhr von Waffen, Drogen und pornografischem Material ist verboten. Nähere Auskünfte finden Sie auch im Travel Centre der IATA. Die angeführten Mengen und Beträge sind unverbindliche Richtangaben, rechtsverbindliche Informationen kann nur die Vertretungsbehörde dieses Landes erteilen. Bitte beachten Sie bei der Einreise nach Österreich die geltenden Einfuhrbestimmungen.

themes.heading: Gesundheit
themes.text: Für die Visabeantragung ist der Nachweis einer Gelbfieberimpfung vorzulegen. Der Impfpass sollte mitgeführt werden. Aufgrund steigender Fallzahlen der Viruserkrankung Mpox (Affenpocken) wird zu erhöhter Vorsicht aufgerufen, sowie empfohlen, Informationen seitens der WHO und ECDC zu beachten. Hygiene, Versorgung mit Medikamenten und Fachpersonal in den Krankenhäusern entsprechen nicht dem europäischen Standard. Es besteht kein Sozialversicherungsabkommen mit Österreich. Es wird darauf hingewiesen, dass die Kosten einer allfälligen Krankenhausbehandlung erheblich sein können. Der Abschluss einer alle Risiken abdeckenden Reiseversicherung wird daher vor Reiseantritt dringend empfohlen. Die Mitnahme einer Reiseapotheke, die nicht nur regelmäßig benötigte Arzneimittel, sondern auch Medikamente für gängige Reiseerkrankungen beinhaltet, wird empfohlen. Wer auf bestimmte Medikamente angewiesen ist, sollte einen ausreichenden Vorrat und einen Nachweis über die ärztliche Verschreibung mitnehmen, auf der Homepage des Sozialministeriums finden Sie nähere Informationen zur Mitnahme von Medikamenten ins Ausland. Es wird empfohlen, rechtzeitig vor Reisebeginn den Hausarzt oder eine andere geeignete Einrichtung zu kontaktieren, um sich über eventuell erforderliche Reiseimpfungen zu erkundigen.

themes.heading: Infrastruktur
themes.text: Zum Lenken eines Kraftfahrzeuges ist der internationale Führerschein in Verbindung mit dem österreichischen Führerschein vorgeschrieben, ein Carnet de Passage ist ebenfalls mitzuführen. An der Grenze muss eine Haftpflichtversicherung abgeschlossen werden. Nähere Informationen zu Verkehrsbestimmungen finden Sie auf der Länderdatenbank des ÖAMTC.

themes.heading: Umwelt
themes.text: Tropisches Klima mit zwei Regenzeiten (Ende September bis Dezember und Mitte Februar bis Ende Mai) und zwei Trockenzeiten, ganzjährig hohe Luftfeuchtigkeit.

themes.heading: Besondere Bestimmungen
themes.text: Foto- und Filmaufnahmen von öffentlichen Einrichtungen (Ministerien, Flughafengebäude, etc.) sind strengstens verboten. Beim Fotografieren von Personen oder Personengruppen sollte grundsätzlich zuerst um deren Einverständnis gefragt werden. Homosexuelle Handlungen werden mit einer Haftstrafe zwischen 3 Monaten und 2 Jahren oder einer Geldstrafe von bis zu 100.000 BIF geahndet. Das Schutzalter für sexuelle Handlungen kann gegenüber den in Österreich geltenden Bestimmungen um einige Jahre höher sein oder sogar über dem Erwachsenenalter von 18 Jahren liegen. Es können jedoch auch (beispielsweise in einzelnen Provinzen oder Regionen) unterschiedliche Bestimmungen zur Anwendung kommen.

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 수도 부줌부라에는 절도, 총기 강도 등 각종 강력범죄 발생이 잦으므로 항상 안전에 유의하여야 합니다. ㅇ 부줌부라 외곽 지역(특히, 부줌부라인근, Bubanza, Citibitoke 등)은 반군들이 여전히 활동하고 있기 때문에 이들 지역으로의 여행은 가급적 삼가주시기 바랍니다. ㅇ 버스 이용시에는 소매치기, 교통사고를 조심하여야 하며, 택시는 영업허가를 받은 정식 택시를 이용하여야 택시강도 피해 위험이 적습니다.

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: ㅇ 도로 바깥쪽 양 끝에 인도와 구분하기 위한 표식이 벽돌 모양으로 바닥에 박혀 있는바, 차량 바퀴가 동 표식의 모서리와 부딪히는 경우 타이어 펑크 등 사고로 이어질 수 있음(공관차량 펑크 사례 있음)

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: ㅇ 교통사고 발생시 즉시 경찰(112)에 연락해야 하며, 교통경찰관에게 받은 조사서(리포트)를 차량보험회사에 제출하면 보상 받을 수 있음

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ 수도인 부줌부라 지역은 비교적 도로 상태가 양호하나, 지방의 경우, 도로 중간에 움푹 파인 곳이 있거나, 비포장도로가 있는 등 도로 정비가 잘 되어 있지 않아 유의해야 함 ㅇ 도로에 가로등이 많지 않아 야간 운전시 특히 주의해야 함

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: ㅇ 시차 : 서울이 7시간 빠름 ㅇ 전기 : 220-240V/50-60HZ(한국 전자제품 사용 가능) ㅇ 부룬디 방문 전 한국에서 황열병 예방접종을 하셔야 합니다.

themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:20 July 2026 Updated:28 May 2026 Latest update:Due to the outbreak of Ebola disease in DRC and Uganda, authorities are conducting body temperature checks on arrival in Burundi. We continue to advise reconsider your need to travel to Burundi overall. Higher levels apply in some areas. There's ongoing armed conflict in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) along the border with Burundi. We advise do not travel to areas bordering the DRC, including Cibitoke and Bubanza provinces, Kibira National Park, and parts of Bujumbura Rural province west of Gatumba. Monitor local media for updates on the security situation. All passengers entering or transiting Burundi must complete an online registration form before travelling (see 'Travel'). There are limited land border options to leave Burundi and fuel shortages are common. The land border with Rwanda is closed (see 'Safety'). We advise: Reconsider your need to travel to Burundi overall due to the threat of terrorism and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Burundi Download Africa

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: There's ongoing armed conflict in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) on the border with Burundi. Do not travel to areas bordering the DRC, including Cibitoke and Bubanza provinces, Kibira National Park, and parts of Bujumbura Rural province west of Gatumba. The security situation is unstable. Violent attacks on individuals are common. Get expert security advice. Terrorism is a threat. Arrange personal security measures and be alert to threats. Violent crime is widespread and often involves weapons. Foreigners can be a target of kidnapping. Be alert to carjacking and other threats. Avoid travelling after dark and take extra precautions if needing to travel during this time. Flooding and mudslides are common from February to June. Infrastructure and essential services may be impacted. Walking and driving in flooded areas is dangerous. Follow the advice of local authorities, check media and weather reports for updates. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Yellow fever is common. You'll need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Burundi. Outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in parts of Africa. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms. HIV/AIDS is widespread. Take precautions to reduce your risk of infection. Serious outbreaks of cholera and malaria can occur. Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common. Use insect repellent. Only drink boiled or bottled water. Mpox cases continue to occur in parts of Africa, including Burundi. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone who has mpox. Health facilities are limited. If you're ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated. Make sure your insurance covers medical evacuation. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Penalties for drug offences are severe and include long prison terms and large fines. It's illegal to take photos of sensitive buildings in Burundi. Don't photograph airports, military sites or government buildings. Same-sex relationships are illegal, and penalties include jail terms. Avoid public displays of affection. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: You need a visa to enter Burundi. This also applies to Burundians living abroad who hold foreign passports. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. All passengers entering or transiting Burundi must register online using the incoming/outgoing passenger form before travelling. You'll receive a certificate that will be required to obtain your visa upon arrival. You'll be required to undertake body temperature checks on arrival, due to the ongoing outbreak of Ebola disease in parts of Africa (see ‘Health’). Borders can close without notice. Burundi's land border with Rwanda continues to remain closed. Landmines have exploded in areas popular with travellers. Road travel is dangerous. Traffic accidents and crime are common. Check local security and road conditions. Get professional security advice. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Burundi. Our ability to provide consular services in Burundi is extremely limited. For consular assistance, contact the Australian High Commission in Nairobi. The Embassy of Belgium in Bujumbura can provide limited assistance to Australians in crisis situations. To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: There are ongoing political tensions in Burundi that can cause sporadic violence. Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent. Violent attacks can happen. You should avoid all large gatherings and remain vigilant. Follow the advice of local authorities. Clashes between police and the public are common in Bujumbura and other parts of Burundi. Outbreaks of violence have also been reported during elections. If you travel to Burundi: get professional security advice use the right personal security measures for the situation avoid protests and other public gatherings and remain vigilant monitor the media for possible unrest keep in contact with your friends and family More information: Demonstrations and civil unrest

themes.heading: Border areas
themes.text: The security situation near the border with the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is volatile and there's a risk of cross-border attacks. Fighting in the area may affect people on the Burundian side of the border. This includes from potential nearby conflict and movement of military and refugees. Keep up to date with developments through local media. We advise do not to travel to Cibitoke and Bubanza provinces, Kibira National Park, and areas of Bujumbura Rural province west of Gatumba that border with the Democratic Republic of Congo due to the threat of armed attacks. Don't cross the border into the DRC. We strongly advise do not travel to the DRC. More information: Travel advice for DRC

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism is a threat. An attack could occur at any time, including places frequented by expatriates and foreigners. Possible targets of terror attacks include: clubs, hotels, resorts, restaurants, bars and tourist areas schools and places of worship markets and shopping centres political, sporting and outdoor recreation events bus terminals, public transport and airports government buildings If despite the risks, you travel to Burundi: arrange personal security measures consider places known to be terrorist targets and the level of security provided always be alert to possible threats monitor the media for new threats take official warnings seriously follow the advice of local authorities If there's an attack nearby, leave the area as soon as it's safe. Avoid the affected area due to the risk of secondary attacks. Terrorism is a threat worldwide. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Violent crime
themes.text: Violent crime is widespread. It often involves weapons. Common types of violent crime include: assault mugging and robbery carjacking in traffic, including ambush by armed criminals on the roads, particularly those leading out of Bujumbura The risk of assault is higher when walking alone on roads around Lake Tanganyika. The risk of crime increases after dark. Avoid walking in the streets or using public transport after dark, even in Bujumbura city centre. The US Embassy advises their staff not to travel during hours of darkness outside Bujumbura Mairie (typically 6pm to 6am). If despite the risks, you travel to Burundi: keep your car windows up and doors locked, even when moving be alert to the threat of carjacking secure your accommodation against intruders be alert to your surroundings don't travel after dark adopt personal security measures keep valuables, travel documents, and cash in a safe location take copies of important documents, including your passport

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological, and criminal motives. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas while travelling. Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. If despite our advice you travel to an area with a high risk of kidnapping, our ability to provide assistance in these destinations is extremely limited. You're responsible for your own safety. You can find more information on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping here: Kidnapping The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Kidnapping

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Burundi can experience extreme weather and natural disasters, including: flooding mudslides earthquakes To protect yourself in the event of a natural disaster or extreme weather: secure your passport in a safe, waterproof location monitor media and other sources for updates follow the advice of local authorities keep in contact with your friends and family

themes.heading: Flooding
themes.text: The rainy season occurs between February and June. During the rainy season, heavy downpours are common and can result in flash floods and mudslides. Flooding may affect the following essential services: transport and infrastructure, including flights electricity supply water and food supplies emergency services To reduce your risks: stay informed of media reporting and regional weather forecast follow the instructions of local authorities avoid the affected areas consider moving to higher ground

themes.heading: Earthquakes
themes.text: Earthquakes can occur in Burundi. Check earthquake response procedures for each place you stay. Register with the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System to receive alerts on major disasters.

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is common in Burundi. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitoes. It's prevented by vaccination. You need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Burundi. Get vaccinated before you travel. Malaria occurs widely, at all times of year. Other diseases spread by insects can occur. This includes: African sleeping sickness chikungunya dengue virus infection filariasis plague zika virus To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medicine to prevent malaria Get medical advice if you have a fever, muscle pain, rash or severe headache.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common. These include: cholera meningococcal disease tuberculosis Rift Valley Fever Serious outbreaks sometimes occur. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids don't swim in fresh water, including at Lake Tanganyika Get medical advice if you have a fever or diarrhoea.

themes.heading: Ebola disease
themes.text: On 17 May, the World Health Organisation (WHO) declared an outbreak of Ebola disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) as a public health emergency of international concern and also reported cases in Uganda. The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola has no approved vaccines or specific antiviral treatments. Ebola can be transmitted to humans through physical contact with an infectious person, infected animals or contaminated materials. Ebola is often fatal with a mortality rate of around 50 per cent. Symptoms of Ebola can include: high fever muscle pain and weakness headache sore throat vomiting and diarrhoea internal and external bleeding Seek medical advice immediately if you experience these symptoms. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms. Health screening on arrival and entry restrictions for travellers who have visited countries affected by Ebola may be imposed with short notice and can change quickly, including for transit passengers. Check the latest requirements with your airline or travel provider before departure. More information: World Health Organisation

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: HIV/AIDS is widespread. Take precautions if you engage in activities that may expose you to the virus.

themes.heading: Mpox
themes.text: Mpox cases continue to occur in parts of Africa, including Burundi. Mpox can be transmitted to humans through physical contact with an infectious person, infected animals or contaminated materials. Seek professional medical advice on mpox vaccines. Maintain strict hygiene standards. Avoid physical contact with anyone who has mpox. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There’s no agreement between Australia and Burundi for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs. Health facilities are basic and limited. If you are ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated to Nairobi, Kenya or another place with better facilities. Medical evacuation can be very expensive. Check that your travel insurance covers medical evacuations.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Penalties for drug offences are severe, including long prison terms and large fines. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: You need a visa to enter Burundi. This also applies to Burundians living abroad who hold foreign passports Visas can be renewed if you're already in Burundi. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact an embassy or consulate of Burundi for details about visas, currency, customs and quarantine rules. Burundi doesn't have diplomatic representation in Australia. The nearest embassy of Burundi is in Tokyo.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: Border closures can occur without notice. The land border with Rwanda has been closed since 2024.

themes.heading: Ebola disease
themes.text: You'll be required to undertake body temperature checks on arrival, due to the outbreak of Ebola disease in parts of Africa.

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Burundi. Some airlines may want to see one when you leave. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Countries with a risk of yellow fever

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with ‘X’ gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Burundian Franc (BIF). You’ll need approval to take more than BIF 200,000 out of the country. Some businesses may accept US dollars or euros. Exchange money only at authorised banks or reputable exchange bureaus to avoid counterfeit issues. Most bureaus may refuse to exchange older notes printed before 2013. ATMs are limited and unreliable. Most hotels and businesses don't accept credit cards. Bank transfers in and out of the country may not be available. Plan ahead. Have enough cash to meet your needs.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Traffic accidents are common. Road safety and driving hazards include: poor condition of most roads and vehicles speeding bad driving habits poor lighting Heavy rains can cause flooding and landslides. These may damage roads and infrastructure. Recurrent fuel shortages and long queues to access fuel are common across the country. Roadblocks occur in all areas, including Bujumbura. Some are formal checkpoints maintained by police, but not all are official. Armed thieves are a significant risk across Burundi. See Safety. If you plan to travel by road: check local security and road conditions get security advice from an expert arrange personal security measures travel in daylight hours travel in a convoy - the US Embassy recommends at least 2 vehicles make sure you have access to enough fuel for your journey and any emergencies carry copies of your passport and visa, though you may be asked to produce the originals at checkpoints Have a contingency plan in case you can't travel by road.

themes.heading: Border areas
themes.text: Border closures can occur without notice. The Burundi land border with Rwanda is currently closed. There have been armed incursions from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) into neighbouring border areas of Burundi, including Cibitoke and Bubanza provinces.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Avoid public transport, especially after dark. The US embassy doesn't allow their staff to use public transportation. There's a high risk of crime, and local roads and vehicles are in a poor state. See Safety.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: Bujumbura international airport can close without warning. DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Check Burundi's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Burundi. For consular help, contact either: Australian High Commission in Nairobi Belgian Embassy in Bujumbura The Belgian Embassy in Bujumbura can provide limited assistance in an emergency. It can't issue Australian passports. You’ll need to register with them by emailing on bujumbura@diplobel.fed.be

themes.heading: Embassy of Belgium, Bujumbura
themes.text: Boulevard de la Liberte, 18 Bujumbura, Burundi Phone: (+257) 2222 6176 or (+257) 79 925 105 in an emergency Email: Bujumbura@diplobel.fed.be Website: Embassy of Belgium in Burundi Check the websites for details about opening hours and any temporary closures.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia
